speaker: interviewer
text: According to the directions, you're supposed to stand next to him and just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, no, no. There's two things that we do. One is the slate, which has to be in front of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, I'm standing next to him after that. I'll show you what we do. Why don't I just slate it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just let me know and I'll turn around and look at you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape is rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is October 23rd, 1995. Liberator Paul Parks, interviewer Naomi Rappaport. The city is Boston, state, Massachusetts, country, USA, and the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. All set.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape, and when I get back, the formal interview does not start until after that, OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Tape is rolling. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Naomi Rappaport and today is October 23rd, 1995. I am conducting an interview with liberator Dr. Paul Parks in Boston, Massachusetts. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Good morning, Dr. Parks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thank you very much for welcoming us and sharing your story and your testimony with the survivors of the Shoah Foundation. I'd like to start with some questions about your name and place of birth and so on. Could you give us your name and spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Paul, P-A-U-L, Parks, P-A-R-K-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Same.

speaker: interviewee
text: Paul Parks. There was another name. [LAUGHS] I'm going to tell you. Translated, it was 'Small Boy.' And so I was known, as a young boy, as 'Small Boy.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was the only other name I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was one nickname that the kids all called me when I was in high-- in grade school and high school, I was known as 'Pap,' P-A-P.

speaker: interviewer
text: P-A-P?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth date.

speaker: interviewee
text: May 7th, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: 72, I would suggest. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Indianapolis, Indiana.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the USA.

speaker: interviewee
text: USA, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to talk about your early life. Your parents-- who were your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- first of all, my father was Cleab Parks, who-- and I don't know where he got his name from.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Cleab?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-L-E-A-B. I don't know how that came about, because he was born on a reservation down in central Florida. He was a Seminole. My mother was part Creek and part Black, was Creek Indian and black. And she was also born on the same reservation with my father. And they came off the reservation in-- around-- right after World War I because my father was offered a job. Someone came to the reservation and told him about all these great jobs they're going to have up in the middle west working in the foundries. And when they quoted the salaries, it looked like an awful lot of money to them. They said they'd pay their way and they'd take them up on the train, which they did. And my fa-- my father and mother went to Marion, Indiana. And he was to work in-- he got a job working in-- in the-- in the iron foundries in Marion as a laborer. And contacted tuberculosis, as many Indians do in that environment. And they moved to Indianapolis because there was a large sanitarium which would treat Indians in Indianapolis. So he went to Indianapolis. And it was at about that time that I was born. My sister, Jacqueline, was the oldest child and she was born. And then, two years after Jacqueline, I was born, and then 18 months later, my sister, Dorothy Jean was born. So there were two girls and a boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year are we talking about that they moved to Indiana?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would suggest, from what I-- I don't know, but I would suggest it was somewhere around 1919, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you at one point indicated that your father had been discharged from World War I?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my father was in World War I, and had-- had been gassed in service. So he was a disabled war veteran, and when he came north again-- he went back to the reservation after the war and then came to Marion. And he was already carrying the problem of some sort of heart condition which he had as a result of the gassing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother-- did your mother work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, when she first came, did not work. But because, I guess, the dire circumstances that they were in because of their lack of money because they found out very quickly that the amount of money they were paying my father really was-- didn't help them to live in the environment they were living in in Marion. So they were living in abject poverty, literally. And my father, who spoke Seminole and could read and write Seminole-- because the Seminoles were one of the few groups-- they called themselves one of the five 'civilized nations.' But he-- they had a written language. So he knew his own language and had been trained in all the other things that happened to Seminoles. But the one thing he didn't have was a-- was a-- was an English education. My mother had-- my mother went back to school, evidently. She was younger than my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she went back to school and finished and then went to-- well, she didn't go back. She went to school in Marion and got-- went as far as getting-- in Indianapolis, I think, when they moved there-- she got a what they call a-- this is like a two-year college degree. A normal school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Normal school. So she finished normal school and went on to become a social worker, and did quite well as a social worker. She was a social worker. It grew and progressively got better as the years went by.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your home, what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [LAUGHS] It was-- I remember growing up on 1051 Road Street in northwest Indianapolis. It was an old-- it was a single-family housing place. My family owned the house. The house that I was born in had no hot and cold running water, and had no central heating. There was an outhouse out in the back and we were in the city. And I remember, one of my mother's objectives was-- I can always remember saying how she was going to get the house a bathroom and a kitchen with a kitchen stove, a gas stove, a refrigerator, and all those things. And she accomplished that. It was a big thing for her and she did it over a period of time. My father was-- my father wanted to-- just wanted to hunt and fish. He really did. That's all he knew. And culturally, that's what he was supposed to do is hunt and fish. So he would go hunting and fishing. And he was-- a little boy, he used to have me tag along with him. And I disliked hunting and fishing, mainly because my mother had sort of proselytized me [LAUGHS] in saying, look, you can't learn-- a boy cannot learn-- hunting and fishing will not cause him  to survive in this world he's in. And he's going to have to go to school. He's going to have to learn to do something other than going hunting and fishing because you can't hunt and fish on these asphalt streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [LAUGHS] my father-- so there was always this conflict. But my father was not a person who talked an awful lot. I've seen my father sit for two days without saying anything to anybody. He just sat. Well, that was all-- when I went to my-- see my father's home, there were a lot of people who sat for days [LAUGHS] without saying anything. So it wasn't unusual for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was rather unusual because he-- my mother would say things to him and he would sort of bow his head, shake his head, and that was it. But he wasn't cruel, you know, or anything else. So, he just didn't have-- he said, the only reason why you talk is when you have something to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what language did he talk to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He talked-- you know, the first-- my first language was French. This is a crazy story. I don't know how to explain this. We spoke French as little kids. Now, my mother finally told me one day that they learned to speak French because there was a missionary-- some missionaries who came to the reservation very early. And they learned to speak French in this little class that they had and-- from the nuns. Now, my mother was a Baptist. My father was a Catholic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my sister was-- my older sister became a Lutheran. So we belonged to all sorts of religious groups because it was not-- it was not the first religion of my family. My family, being Seminoles, had the religion of Native Americans at the time, and all the rest of these things were sort of superimposed on us. So they learned to live compatibly with it, but I don't think they learned to accept it or take it in as a-- as a-- as a deep, intrinsic, philosophical thing for themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the Seminole religion practiced in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we still believe that the great spirit was in the trees, in the forest. We believe that-- and basically, you believe that all human beings are human beings, and that there is no difference in people, regardless of what they look like, as long as they're human beings. And all human beings are alike. And that the animals of the world are-- are not far from human beings. They interact with human beings, and they-- they do things to help human beings. And some of them were sacred to us. Eagles were sacred. And there were some other things that I don't remember now that were sacred to us. But the idea of God being in the trees and all the great father being in the trees and being in the-- in the grass and being-- and that one ought to always respect the natural things around us and not to destroy them wantonly. One of the great tragedies I think the Indians suffered in America was the Indians believe you live-- whatever-- whatever mother nature delivers up, you live-- you try to live compatible with it. You don't change it. You don't sweep it away. As opposed to the Europeans who said, whatever there is, we will change it so that our life quality will be better and we can live more comfortably. And I think that was the great schism between the American Indians, the Native Americans, and the whites who came in from Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a Baptist, which you said you were, was there much conflict between the religion-- the Seminole religion that you were practicing at home and the church?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was. My father would never go to church. [LAUGHS] My mother used to try to convince him to come to church with us because she would take us. And I think the reason why we were Baptist wasn't because that was a-- that was a basic ideology called Baptist. What happened was that was the church that was in walking distance of our home. And my mother used to walk us around the corner, because we had no transportation other than walking. So my mother used to walk us to the church, which was in our neighborhood. And it was a Baptist church, and it was a church in the basement because they never were able to get the money to build the church above the basement.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to go to this little basement church. But my father refused to go because he saw it as being very sacrilegious for him to become-- to come to this kind of religion. And he just wouldn't do it. The-- to get back to the house--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in which you lived, how large was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see. There were two bedrooms and-- upstairs. It was a two-story house-- two bedrooms upstairs and one, two, three, four rooms downstairs. It was-- we-- when I was a small child, we only had furniture in the kitchen and a small table in the living room, and that was about it. And we had a kitchen stove that was a coal stove, or a wood stove, as the case may be, whatever you had. And it served as heat as well as cooking. Many of the other rooms in the house didn't have any heat, particularly. But we used to go between them but there never was heat. Our water was outside at a pump, or a well was outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: The area, was that-- who lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was-- it's interesting. It was a totally black neighborhood. I would saw the police were black. The firemen were black. The only people we saw that weren't black-- and I'll come to one family. There was one family who lived in the neighborhood-- there were two families, literally, who lived in the neighborhood who were white. Well, white-- one was Jewish. The other-- the only other white person we ever saw was the Metropolitan Insurance man and the visiting nurse. But the one family that was white had a son, Daniel, who was-- we became-- he and I became very good friends from little boys, because we were the two oddballs in the community. People saw, you know, I'm a Native American. Well, I'm with a Native American family, and we lived-- our lifestyle was different than other people in the community. So we were constantly being harassed, if you please. People made fun of us. They did all kinds. And so I was in a fight every day going to grade school with somebody who was called-- my sister would say, he called me a name. And I'd wait for him after school and attack him, literally. [LAUGHS] Don't go saying anything about my sisters, and-- because I was trained to always protect the women in my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I did that as best I could. The principal, one day, told me, you can't beat everybody. You've got to learn to live with folks. And I said, I'm not trying to beat everybody. I'm just trying to beat all those who do something to me or my family. [LAUGHS] Did Daniel go to school with you, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Daniel did not go to school with me. It's very interesting. Daniel went to the-- they bussed Daniel to a school outside the neighborhood. But Daniel and I used to stand back to back and fight all the kids in the neighborhood [INAUDIBLE] when they bothered us, because they were-- he was under attack and I was under attack, and we became very good friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Daniel was Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Daniel was-- No, he was white.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was white but not Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, not Jewish. Now, the Jewish family was Gabe Segal, the grocery up at the corner. He owned the little grocery store in the community. He and his wife and two children lived in the back of the market. And he's-- he's a very interesting story because, had it not been for him, I don't think my family would have survived the Depression. Mr. Segal-- my mother used to go and talk to Mr. Segal, and Mr. Segal had a little book that he would-- we would go-- send me to-- my mother would send me to the grocery when I was-- I remember going to the grocery. And Mr. Segal would put it down in his little book, what I got, and took it back home. And whenever my mother could pay him, she would pay him. Sometimes, she could pay it all. Sometimes, she paid part of it. And Mr. Segal never, ever harassed her. He was the nicest man that I ever knew. He-- you know, at that time, he was just-- we just would not have eaten. We would not have survived if it had not been for Mr. Segal. And Mr. Segal-- that's why I say, when I grew up, there were three races. There was white, there was-- there was Negroes, and there was Jews, the three races. And I never saw Mr. Segal as being white or black. I just didn't see him. He was just Jewish. And nobody ever robbed Mr. Segal. Nobody ever bothered his family. Nobody ever touched him because there were other people in the neighborhood that he helped to survive also. Now, how that man managed to live himself I will never know. I know he lived in the back of his grocery store. When I went to college, we were-- when I went to college, my mother had very little money. I won a scholarship to go to college. And I-- it was a $4,000 scholarship because I won the National American Legion Oratorical Contest. And-- but I couldn't-- I wanted to come to MIT at the time, and I couldn't come because we didn't have enough money for me to even ride on the train to come to-- to come to Boston.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mentor, who I will talk about that later-- but my mentor got me to go to Purdue. I have-- The scholarship would only pay for the tuition. The room and board we had to find a way to pay for ourselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember, the first year I was in-- the first year I was in Purdue, Mr. Segal was the person who kept me there. He paid for-- he gave my mother the money to-- to pay for my room and board, and I survived. And it was just him. He never said anything about it. [INAUDIBLE] When I was a kid-- when I was a kid, about eight years old, he gave me a job plucking feathers off of chickens because, you know, in our community, everybody had fried chicken every Sunday. So people bought a lot of chickens, and I took the feathers off the chicken and he showed me how to do it. And he gave me a little job doing that. And I got to know his-- his daughter and his son very well, because they were a little younger than me. But the whole family was just very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did those children go to the same school as you, as children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They went to the Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see. The school you went to, who were your friends there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- when I-- you mean at grade school?

speaker: interviewer
text: At grade school, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were several people that I finally became friends with. Fellow-- there was a fellow named Jack Gardner who-- who later-- later became the associate dean at the Medical school at Rutgers. There was a fellow named Tom Waller who, we were inseparable for a while. And he was-- he became a police officer in Chicago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, there were several-- several other people that I knew but those were the two fellows that I was closest-- that I was closest with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the school a black--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh and another fellow.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a fellow, Maurice [? Neisler. ?] And [LAUGHS] my mother was never comfortable with that relationship, mainly because she saw Maurice as a person who would get me into trouble, not from a standpoint of the street kind of trouble, but because I was always trying to protect him from people assaulting him. And he was one of those kids that everybody wanted to just chase for some reason, and I was always the guy there stopping them. [LAUGHS] And we have a relationship to this day. We both went to Purdue together. His family was-- was much better off, economically, than my family was, and they lived up the street from us and lived in a very nice house and all this sort of thing. And we just became good friends, and my mother said a friendship is my protector. I was the protector of him. [LAUGHS] So-- But it was an all-black school. All the teachers were black. And interesting enough, Indianapolis was a very unusual place, because Indianapolis had integrated schools. It was the black community that asked for segregated schools. And the reason why they wanted their schools to be all-black is they wanted a place where they-- where the people who they were getting-- who were going to college could come back and teach in the schools. So our teachers were black. Our principals were black. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Even our superintendent-- deputy superintendent was black. So all of the-- all of our education that we saw was all black folk, all the way through high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to go back to your father for one moment. Did he work at all, at anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my father worked at several things. First of all, when he came out the army, they trained him to be a tailor. I  don't think my father was ever the best tailor in the world, but he had a little shop for a while. When he first came to Indianapolis, while he was recuperating from his tuberculosis, he had a little tailor shop. Now, my mother always felt that that was the wrong place because she felt that lint from the clothing would exacerbate his tuberculosis. But-- and then finally, he got sick doing it. But he did that. And then, when he closed his little tailor shop, he was a chauffeur for a very wealthy family, the Lilly family, which is still around-- Lilly Pharmaceuticals. And he was also a banker, someone who's pressing one of the banks in Boston-- he was-- I mean, Indianapolis. He was a chauffeur for them. And so he used to take me with him when I was old enough, and we'd cut-- and I would cut the grass and I would help him cut the grass at these various places.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother continued to work as a social worker?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, at that time, was just beginning her work as a social worker when I was little. And she was working at the welfare office where-- you know, the welfare office in those days was different than it is now. What they did mostly was they provided the clothing for people who couldn't afford the clothing during the Depression. So she was working at the place where they gave out the clothing for people to-- for the people who couldn't afford to buy clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like on the streets? Was there much diversity on your street, in terms of friendships, games, interests?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family-- there was one person across the street from us, a woman who was a teacher in the public school, Mrs. Washington, who my mother talked to periodically. But we had very few people who came to our house to visit and things of that sort. We were sort of the outside group, so there were very few-- there were very few relationships on a personal basis between my-- my uncles, my-- my mother's brothers-- two brothers-- and her sister all moved to Indianapolis. And my father's brothers and sisters, several of his brothers and sisters, moved to Indianapolis.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was pretty much the relationships that we had was a family relationship because the people in the neighborhood we just very seldom-- that was one-- I don't how we got to know, but there was another man who my mother and father got to know from somewhere, who was from India. And he used to come and have lunch with-- dinner with us on Sunday many times. And he was a very close friend of the family. But that's about the only person.

speaker: interviewee
text: The kids we knew, I got to know a lot of the kids because, at some point, I was a-- I got to be a part of a-- I was a part of a gang, I guess, is the best way to describe it. And I became the leader of this group of teenagers-- and we were early teens-- which was finally broken up by-- by the police and by some people at the high school finally broke us up. But I was involved in that for a while. And I think the only reason why I was a part of this gang wasn't because-- was because, once again, it was a way which I was able to survive in a community that was very hostile toward us. But once I was head of the gang, nobody bothered me. The kids left me alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what kind of books did you read as a kid growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's interesting. Now, the one thing I did, my mother, when we were very small, she would take us to the library. We'd walk to the library. It was about five blocks away from our house. And we would get all kinds of books. I read-- I read-- I think one summer, I must've read 50 books. We were-- she just kept us reading, and that's how I learned about the rest of the world. I could-- you know, it was-- it was a beautiful real thing to do. I could read a book and go off to-- to England. I could go off to Europe. I could go off to Africa. I could go off to a lot of places. I could-- I could dream of myself being a part of the French and Indian War. Whatever I was reading, I had this great ability in my own mind to place myself, as a little boy, and daydream about my role in all these kinds of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about newspapers? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother always had a newspaper coming to the house, but she'd buy it. But we always had, today, two daily papers, a morning and evening paper, because she said that you-- that's the only way that you'll ever learn was to-- about America, about this country we're in, is to read. And-- and so we had the paper. And my mother used to tell me-- as a little boy, she said-- I learned every state in the union. I learned the capital of every state. I learned the total presidential cabinet. I learned the president. [LAUGHS] I learned all of them. And I had to do it, because when I got up in the morning and come downstairs, she would ask me a question. And the thing that our family did, which was also part of it, we would all go to my grandmother. My mother's mother was there now. We would all go to my grandmother's every Saturday. We'd get dressed and go to my grandmother's, and we'd debate issues all day long. We'd just stand there and somebody would raise something. And when they'd finally get something everybody turned on about, we'd discuss it all day. The little kids and everybody used to step up and discuss.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sisters were as involved as you?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of us were involved, everybody. All of us read. All of us did those kinds of things. So culturally, it was amazing. I always say that I was-- I was-- I was poor. I was without money. But I was never poor in spirit.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.
